title: Chapter 11: The Serpent's Last Strike
content: House arrest did not suit Lady Isolde Vane.

For three days, she had been confined to her chambers in the noble wing — not a dungeon, because her family's political influence demanded a certain level of courtesy, but not freedom either. Guards at her door. Meals brought on trays. No visitors except her steward, who had already been replaced by one of Darius's most trusted wolves.
Sera knew this because Kael had told her. And because Fenris had spent approximately forty percent of his mental energy composing increasingly creative revenge fantasies.

*"We should exile her to the northern wastes. No — the SOUTHERN wastes. Are there southern wastes? There should be southern wastes. I'll HAVE some wastes made. Specifically for Isolde. 'Isolde's Personal Wasteland.' Very exclusive. No amenities."*
But Isolde, it turned out, had other plans.
The summons came on the morning of the fourth day. A formal challenge — written on noble parchment, sealed with the Vane crest, delivered to the Alpha's study by Isolde's remaining loyal servant.
Kael read it twice. His expression didn't change, but Sera heard the shift in his mind — the cold, sharp focus of a predator who had just spotted prey making a fatal mistake.
"She's invoking the Right of Trial," he said.
"The what?"

*"Ancient pack law. Older than the castle. Older than most of our traditions. If a noble believes they have been wrongfully accused, they can demand a public trial by combat — or by ordeal. It's a gamble. If she wins, she's proven innocent and we can't touch her again. If she loses..."*
"She dies," Sera finished. "Or whatever the trial demands."

"Exile, usually. Sometimes death, depending on the crime." Kael set the parchment down. "She must be desperate. House arrest is damaging her family's reputation, but a public trial could destroy her completely. The fact that she's willing to risk it means she has something up her sleeve."
"A champion," Sera said. "She'll call a champion to fight in her place. Some noble who owes her family a favor. Someone strong enough to defeat..."
She stopped.
"Someone strong enough to defeat *you*," Kael finished. "Because as the accuser, I would have to face her champion personally."
"That's insane. You're the Alpha. You're the strongest wolf in the pack."

"I'm the strongest wolf in the pack *who plays by the rules*." Kael's golden eyes were hard. "The Vane family has money. Connections. They could have hired someone from outside the pack — a mercenary, a rogue Alpha from another territory. Someone who doesn't care about pack law or fair fights."

*"She's trying to KILL him. Not defeat him — KILL him. A public trial where the Alpha dies in 'fair combat' — there would be no repercussions. The Vane family could claim it was the Goddess's judgment. They could even push for Isolde to become Alpha."*
"Can you refuse?" Sera asked.

"I can. But if I do, Isolde walks free. The challenge is public now — the nobles already know about it. If I refuse to face her champion, it looks like cowardice. Like I'm afraid to prove my accusations."
"Which would destroy your authority."
"Exactly."
Sera's mind raced. "What if you're not the one who faces her?"
Kael frowned. "The Right of Trial specifies that the accuser must — "
"The accuser is the one who brings the charges, right? Who formally accused Isolde of conspiracy?"
"You did," Kael said slowly. "At the servants' interrogation. Your testimony is what triggered the arrest."
"Then I'm the accuser." Sera met his eyes. "Let me face her champion."

*"NO. Absolutely NOT. NO. She's been a wolf for less than TWO WEEKS. She's barely learned to transform on command. She's — she's INCREDIBLE and BRAVE and the most amazing person I've ever met but she is NOT fighting a mercenary Alpha in a TRIAL BY COMBAT. Kael, TELL HER NO."*
"No," Kael said.
"Kael — "

"No, Sera." His voice was iron. "I've spent my entire adult life being the one who fights. The one who bleeds. The one who stands between this pack and the things that want to destroy it. I am not going to let you — "
"I'm not asking for permission."
The words hung in the air between them.

"I'm not a maid anymore," Sera said quietly. "I'm not the wolf-less girl who flinches when people are mean to her. I'm Moon-Marked. I'm the Silver Queen of the prophecy. And if I'm going to claim my place — if I'm going to stand beside you the way the prophecy says I will — then I need to *prove* it. Not to you. To the pack."
"The pack already knows — "

"The pack is afraid of me. Half of them think I'm a curse. The other half think I'm your pet. Neither half respects me." She stepped closer, reaching up to touch his face. "I need them to see me fight. I need them to see me *win*. I need to claim what's mine."
Kael's jaw was so tight she could hear his teeth grinding.

*"She's right. I hate that she's right. I HATE it. But she's RIGHT. The prophecy said she has to claim the crown. She can't do that hiding behind us. She has to do it in front of everyone, in a way they can't deny."*
"What if you lose?" Kael's voice was barely a whisper.
"Then I die, and you avenge me." Sera's smile was small and fierce. "But I'm not going to lose."
"How can you be sure?"
"Because I can hear everything Isolde's champion is thinking. Every strategy. Every weakness. Every moment of doubt." She tapped her temple. "And because I have something worth fighting for."
She rose on her toes and kissed him — brief but fierce.
"Trust me."
Kael closed his eyes. When he opened them, the iron was still there, but underneath it was something else. Something that looked a lot like faith.
"If you die," he said, "I'm going to be very annoyed."
"Noted."

*"If she dies, I'm going to burn this entire castle to the ground and then howl at the moon until the moon falls out of the sky. But she's not going to die. She's not going to die because she's SERAPHINA VALE and she's the most stubborn, brilliant, infuriating woman I've ever met and she's going to WIN this trial and then I'm going to MARRY her so HARD."*
Sera's smile widened. "Fenris is planning the wedding again."
"Of course he is."
The trial was set for the next full moon — three days away.

Isolde's champion was announced that evening: a rogue Alpha from the eastern territories, a wolf called Grimshaw who had been exiled from his own pack for excessive violence and had spent the last five years selling his claws to the highest bidder.
The pack was terrified of him.

Grimshaw was enormous — even bigger than Kael in wolf form — with a pelt the color of dried blood and a reputation for killing his opponents slowly. He had never lost a trial by combat. He had never even been seriously injured.
And in three days, Sera was going to have to face him.
"You're pale," Magnus observed when Sera arrived at his tower for training that evening.
"I'm about to fight a murder-machine in front of the entire pack. I think pale is appropriate."

"The prophecy didn't specify that you would win easily. Only that you would claim what is yours." Magnus's pale eyes were knowing. "Trials are not about strength alone. They are about worthiness. About proving that you deserve to stand where you stand."
"How do I prove that against a wolf twice my size?"

"By being smarter. Faster. By using every tool at your disposal." Magnus paused. "By trusting the bond you share with the Alpha. Your wolf draws strength from that bond — you've felt it. When you fought the rogues, you were fighting to protect him. That's when your wolf is strongest."
"Love-powered superpowers," Sera muttered. "It sounds like a terrible romance novel."
"The Goddess has a flair for the dramatic." Magnus's beard twitched. "Always has."
That night, Sera couldn't sleep.

She stood at the window of her servant's quarters — she'd refused to move to the noble wing, and Kael had given up arguing — watching the moon wax toward fullness. Three more days until it was round and bright. Three more days until she had to face a monster in front of everyone she'd ever known.
A knock at her door.
She knew who it was before she opened it. The mind-link told her — a storm of worry and determination and stubborn, fierce protectiveness.
"You should be resting," Kael said when she opened the door.
"So should you."
"I never rest." He stepped into her room — the first time he'd ever been inside it, she realized. The first time the Alpha had ever set foot in the servant quarters. "You don't have to do this."
"We've had this argument."
"We're having it again." He caught her hands in his. "Sera. If something happens to you — "
"Nothing's going to happen."
"You don't know that."

"Neither do you." She squeezed his hands. "But I know what I can do. I've been training. My wolf is stronger every day. And I'll be able to hear Grimshaw's thoughts during the fight — every attack before he makes it, every weakness he tries to hide."
"The link works on him?"
"I don't know. It worked on the servants during the interrogation — sort of. I could feel their emotions, even if I couldn't hear specific thoughts. If I can feel Grimshaw's intentions..."
"Then you'll know what he's going to do before he does it."
"Exactly."
Kael was silent for a long moment. Then he pulled her into his arms — sudden and fierce, burying his face in her hair.
"I can't lose you," he said, his voice muffled. "I've only just found you."

*"I can't lose her. I CAN'T. She's — she's everything. She's the reason I'm learning to be whole. She's the reason Fenris and I are starting to understand each other. She's the reason I believe the prophecy might actually come true. If she dies..."*
"I'm not going to die." Sera wrapped her arms around him. "I'm going to win. I'm going to claim my place. And then I'm going to come back here and kiss you until Fenris runs out of poetry."
A rough sound that might have been a laugh. "He never runs out of poetry."
"Then I have my work cut out for me."
Kael pulled back just enough to look at her face. His golden eyes were molten — not with cold authority, but with something raw and real and terrifyingly vulnerable.
"I love you."
The words fell between them like stones into still water.
Sera stopped breathing.

"I know you can hear it," Kael said, his voice rough. "I know you've been hearing it for weeks, in Fenris's voice and in mine. But I needed to say it. Out loud. So that you know — whatever happens in that trial — you are not fighting alone. You have never been fighting alone."
*"KAEL. YOU SAID IT. YOU ACTUALLY SAID IT. THE THREE WORDS. THE BIG THREE. I'M SO PROUD OF YOU I COULD EXPLODE. I'M ACTUALLY GOING TO EXPLODE. THIS IS AN EXPLOSION OF PRIDE AND LOVE AND JOY AND — "*
Sera kissed him.
It was not gentle. It was not brief. It was the kiss of a woman who had just heard the three words she'd been waiting for and had no intention of letting them go unanswered.
"I love you too," she said against his mouth. "In case that wasn't clear."
"It was clear."
"Good." She kissed him again. "Now stop worrying and let me win my trial."
Kael's laugh was the warmest sound she'd ever heard.
"As my lady commands."
